what_it_does: A bash script implementing 'Gate D' of the claude-design connector verification: it mints a design_client JWT for the throwaway design-test user and calls the Edge Function's `channels` history op on the 'design' channel, then pretty-prints each post's from_session, sender_kind, and text. The purpose is to confirm that RLS lets the design client read both its own posts and the fork/company-side posts in the shared channel, proving two-way participation on the same data.
observations: Header marks it as GATE D, to be run AFTER the fork posts a company message to 'design' from a non-claude-design session
Embeds the anon JWT (iss supabase-demo) and the test creds design-test@company.local / TestPass123!
Mints token via password grant, fails fast if empty
Calls tools/call name=channels op=history channel=design via JSON-RPC 2.0
Inline python3 parses the result content text, JSON-decodes the rows, and prints from_session | sender_kind | text[:60] per post
Expectation stated: BOTH from_session=claude-design (CD) and from_session=<company session> (fork) appear
standouts: Frames the test as proving 'same data, two-way participation' — both principals see the same channel rows via RLS
A single Edge Function history call drives the whole verification
conventions: Lettered gate labeling (Gate D), complementing gates A–H in .cd-drive.sh
JSON-RPC tools/call envelope for the MCP Edge Function
Throwaway-creds + gitignored discipline stated in header
concerns: Embeds the test password and anon JWT in plaintext (mitigated by local demo stack, gitignored per header)
The inline python parsing has a complex ternary expression that is hard to read/maintain
notes: Gate-D companion script: reads the shared 'design' channel history as the design client to prove RLS grants two-way visibility of both CD and fork posts.
questions: Which company session posts the fork-side message that this gate expects to see?
What is sender_kind and what values distinguish CD vs fork posts?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: header describes reading design-channel history as the design client to prove two-way visibility, and the body performs exactly that.
apparent_themes: RLS-scoped shared visibility (same rows, two principals)
Verification by USE against a live stack
Two-way cross-principal participation in a shared channel
intention_signals: Trying to empirically prove that the design client and the company-side fork participate in the same 'design' channel and see the same posts, by reading history through the RLS-scoped Edge Function.
novelty: The cross-principal same-data proof — reading as one party to confirm the other party's post is visible in the same shared channel row set.
contribution: Contributes the Gate-D verification step: an executable check that the design client can read the shared 'design' channel history including fork-side posts via RLS.
summary_for_embedding: Bash Gate-D script reading the shared 'design' channel history as the design_client to prove RLS-scoped two-way post visibility.
intention_for_embedding: To verify that the design client and the company fork see the same posts in the shared design channel through RLS.
